titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Henry Hicks Gallery collection CBHM.0037
titlestmt.author: Alice Griffin

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2026-04-23 15:30:52 +0000.
profiledesc.langusage: Description is written in: English, Latin script.

did.abstract: The Henry Hicks Gallery collection, 1975-1981, contains materials related to the Henry Hicks Gallery, a cooperative artist-run gallery in Brooklyn Heights on Atlantic Avenue. Materials include photographs, newsletters, promotional mailings and flyers, and catalogs.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Henry Hick Gallery collection, 1975-1981, includes materials related to Henry Hicks Gallery, a cooperative artist-run gallery formerly at 93 Atlantic Avenue. The collection includes photographs, likely of an art opening; newsletters and magazines published by Henry Hicks Gallery and other artist organizations; and Henry Hicks Gallery exhibition and event flyers.
Most of the exhibition and event flyers are mock-ups with original ink drawings by co-founders David Cole and Roger Erickson.

The two Henry Hicks Gallery publications Henry Hicks Newsletter and Henry Hicks Quick Art Review include announcements of upcoming shows, artist profiles, and writings and drawings by Cole and Erickson. Volume 1, No. 3 of Henry Hicks Newsletter includes a work by Bob Heman.

The Report from Brooklyn published by the Brooklyn Arts and Culture Association (BACA), describes the BACA/YWCA Artists' Project, a project funded under the Comprehensive Employment and Training Act (CETA), and includes testimonials from artists.

The Artists Review Art publications are published by the Association of Artist-Run Galleries (AARG) and feature artists' reviews of gallery shows. Their special issues on Artists' Day in 1976 and 1977 are also included. Artists' Day was an annual festival sponsored by the AARG, which spanned galleries in Brooklyn and Manhattan.

bioghist.head: Historical Note
bioghist.p: Henry Hicks Gallery was founded by artists David Cole and Roger Erickson. Cole was a correspondence artist and artists' book creator. He also organized the first exhibition of concrete poetry in New York City in February 1976 at Henry Hicks Gallery. Erickson was also project administrator of the Brooklyn Arts and Culture Association/YWCA Artists' Project.

The gallery was located at 93 Atlantic Avenue, between Henry and Hicks Streets, and part of a row of cooperative galleries on Atlantic Avenue in Brooklyn Heights, which bacame known as "Art Row," "Renaissance Road," and "Grand Gallery." Other galleries were Atlantic Gallery, Iama Gallery, Gallery 91, Sixth Estate Gallery, and Works of Art Gallery. An exhibition featuring some of the work from these galleries opened in the Community Gallery of the Brooklyn Museum in December 1975.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: While many items at the Center for Brooklyn History are unrestricted, we do not own reproduction rights to all materials. Be aware of the several kinds of rights that might apply: copyright, licensing and trademarks. The researcher assumes all responsibility for copyright questions.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Gift of Simon and Alexa Cole, 2025.

phystech.head: Physical Characteristics and Technical Requirements
phystech.p: If digital surrogates exist, they should be used in place of the originals whenever possible.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Identification of item, date (if known); Henry Hicks Gallery collection, CBHM.0037, Box and Folder number; Brooklyn Public Library, Center for Brooklyn History.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: All folder titles were provided by the archivist.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: The Center for Brooklyn History holds the following related collections:
- Brooklyn Arts and Culture Association publications, 1969-1997 (ARC.149)
The Franklin Furnace Archive has artist files related to David Cole and Roger Erickson.

did.unittitle: Report from Brooklyn, Brooklyn Arts and Culture Association
